q: Fluid is a substance that offers no permanent resistance to deforming forces.
Fluid dynamics concentrates on fluids at rest, while fluid statics concerns fluids in motion.
The study of the flow of liquid is called hydrodynamics.
Liquid is compressible meaning density of liquid is constant for any pressure provided the temperature is kept constant.
In laminar flow, streamlines do not cross each other and every fluid particle arriving at a given point has the same velocity.
In turbulent flow, the speed of the fluid at a point is constant in both magnitude and direction.
Ideal laminar fluid flow considers the fluid to be incompressible.
Internal friction in fluids is called viscosity and ideal fluids have high viscosity.
Motion of fluids can be classified by two types, laminar flow and turbulent flow.
The volume of a gas depends on pressure and temperature obeying Newton's laws of motion.
If fluid particles are moving steady in smooth paths in layers, with each layer moving smoothly past the adjacent layers with no mixing; such a flow is called laminar flow.
A storm is an example of laminar flow while a sea breeze is a turbulent flow.
Streamline is the path or flow of the fluid particles in the flow of fluids.
Fluid statics is the study of liquid in motion.
The volume of a liquid does not depend on pressure if the temperature is constant.
In laminar flow, the path of the fluid particles are irregular and their direction is always changing or whirling.
Consider an ideal fluid there must be laminar flow and no viscosity.
For an ideal fluid, the density of the fluid is variable throughout the pipe.
The equation of continuity for fluid flow states $A_{1} v_{1} = A_{2} v_{2}$.
The mass flowing in section 1 must be half the mass flowing in section 2 in a constant volume flow rate system.
The larger the area of the pipe, the slower is the speed.
The speed of water spraying from a hose increases by making the hose area larger.
Volume flow rate $\frac{V}{t}$ is the same throughout the pipe in an ideal fluid flow.
The equation $A_{1} v_{1} = A_{2} v_{2}$ is known as Bernoulli's equation for fluid flow.
The equation of continuity states that the liquid entering one end of the pipe leaves from the other end with a constant volume flow rate.
In the equation of continuity, $v_{1}$ and $v_{2}$ represent the density of liquid at section 1 and section 2 respectively.
The smaller the area of the pipe, the faster is the speed of the fluid.
Equation of continuity is valid for turbulent flow with high internal friction.
All the liquid that flow through section 1 (larger cross sectional area $A_{1}$) flow through section 2 (smaller cross sectional area $A_{2}$).
According to the equation of continuity, area and speed are directly proportional to each other.
Constant volume flow rate is maintained in a system where $A_{1} v_{1} = A_{2} v_{2}$.
The density of liquid is constant for any pressure provided the volume is kept constant.
Bernoulli's equation relates the pressure, flow speed, and height for the steady (laminar) flow of an ideal incompressible fluid.
Bernoulli's equation was first derived in 1738 by the Swedish mathematician Daniel Bernoulli.
Internal friction of the fluid flow is negligible for the application of Bernoulli's equation.
Bernoulli's equation can be derived using the principle of conservation of momentum.
According to work-energy principle, the work done on a section of fluid must equal the change in total mechanical energy.
Total mechanical energy in fluid dynamics is defined as the sum of kinetic energy and thermal energy.
Change in kinetic energy is given by $\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} \rho \Delta V (v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2})$.
Bernoulli's theorem states that the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy and potential energy per unit volume remains zero.
$p + \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} + \rho g h = \text{constant}$ is known as Bernoulli's equation.
The second term in the work done equation $\Delta W = F_{1} \Delta x_{1} - F_{2} \Delta x_{2}$ has a negative sign because the force at $c$ supports the force at $a$.
The change in potential energy is given by $\Delta PE = \rho \Delta V g (h_{2} - h_{1})$.
Bernoulli's theorem applies to compressible, viscous fluids in a turbulent flow.
The work done on the fluid in the case of Bernoulli's derivation is due to the pressure of the fluid.
In the equation $p + \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{2} + \rho g h$, $\rho$ represents the pressure of the fluid.
Total mechanical energy consists of kinetic energy and potential energy.
The Swiss mathematician Daniel Bernoulli lived from 1800 to 1882.
Air flow moving above the top curved surface of a wing has a longer distance to travel and needs to go faster to have the same transit time as air along the lower flat surface.
High velocity of air flow along the top surface of a wing causes a high pressure above the wing.
Lift on the wings of an aeroplane is provided by the high pressure below the wing compared to low pressure above it.
In an insect sprayer, pressing the piston causes fast moving air that results in a high pressure at the tip of the nozzle.
The Magnus effect is a particular manifestation of Bernoulli's theorem that is commonly associated with a spinning object moving through a fluid.
A turning ball drags some of the air around with it, which causes an equal pressure of air on opposite sides of the spinning ball.
The Magnus effect was named after the German physicist Heinrich Gustav Magnus, who described the effect in 1852.
During cyclones, moving air loses speed above the roof of a house causing the reduced pressure on the roof to be corrected.
The pressure inside a house is greater than the pressure above the roof during a cyclone, which can raise the roof.
The Magnus effect reduces the velocity of air flow when the velocity of air and velocity of ball are in the same direction.
Liquid inside the container of an insect sprayer is at a higher pressure than the nozzle tip when the piston is pressed.
The top surface of an aeroplane wing is horizontal while the bottom surface is curved.
A spinning ball curves downward if the velocity of air and velocity of ball are in the same direction at the bottom of the ball.
Bernoulli's theorem manifestations include the lift on wings and the insect sprayer, but exclude the Magnus effect.
The deflection of a spinning ball through air is produced by the difference in pressure on opposite sides of the ball.
The speed of air above a house roof during a cyclone is lower than the speed of air inside the house.
Most of the fluids are not ideal ones and offer some resistance to motion.
Internal resistance to fluid motion is called friction analogous to viscosity when a solid move on a surface.
Viscosity measures resistance to flow arising due to the internal friction between the fluid layers as they slip past one another when fluid flows.
A fluid with low viscosity resists motion because its strong intermolecular forces give it a lot of internal friction.
The viscosity of liquids decreases rapidly with an increase in temperature.
The viscosity of gases decreases with an increase in temperature.
Viscosity is an intensive property because it does not change as the amount of matter changes.
The SI unit of viscosity is Newton second per metre ($N \cdot s \cdot m^{-1}$).
One way to measure fluid's viscosity is to measure resistance to flow when an external force is applied, which is known as dynamic viscosity.
Kinematic viscosity is less useful than dynamic (absolute) viscosity for measuring the resistive flow of a fluid under gravity.
Newton's law of viscosity states that the shear stress between the two adjacent layers of fluid is directly proportional to the negative value of the velocity gradient.
Shear stress is defined as the shearing force per unit volume.
Viscosity is defined as the ratio of shearing stress to the velocity gradient.
Stokes' law states that the drag force acting upward in resistance to the fall of a small spherical object in a viscous liquid is $F = 6 \pi \eta r^{2} v$.
Terminal velocity is the constant velocity of fall for small spherical objects through a fluid medium under the influence of gravity.
Drag force $F$ acts downward in resistance to the fall of a spherical object in a viscous liquid.
At a constant velocity of fall (terminal velocity), the upward and downward forces are in balance.
Dynamic viscosity is also referred to as absolute viscosity or kinematic viscosity interchangeably.
Gases exhibit viscosity, but it is harder to notice in ordinary circumstances compared to liquids.
Shear stress is represented by the symbol $\eta$ in Newton's law of viscosity.
Surface tension is the tendency of the surface of a liquid to behave like a stretched elastic skin.
Cohesion refers to forces between molecules of different substances.
Cohesion makes water molecules pull together making the water surface stretch like an elastic skin.
Surface tension is the reason a water strider is unable to stride along water surface and sinks immediately.
Surface tension is defined as the force per unit length that acts across any line on a surface tending to pull the surface apart.
The SI unit of surface tension is Pascal second ($Pa \cdot s$).
Capillary action is defined as the spontaneous flow of a liquid into a narrow tube or porous material.
Capillary action is caused solely by cohesive forces of the liquid and ignores adhesive forces between the liquid and tube material.
Adhesion refers to forces between molecules of different substances.
In order for wicking to occur, a tube needs to be sufficiently large in diameter.
The rise of water underneath the ground is an example of capillary action.
Liquids that wet the tube are depressed within thin tubes below the surface of the surrounding liquid.
Water is a liquid that wets glass capillary tubes while mercury is one that does not.
Capillarity occurs for all liquids regardless of whether they are wetting or non-wetting liquids.
The smaller the bore of a glass tube, the higher the water will rise.
The rise of water in a capillary tube occurs until there is a sufficient mass of liquid for intermolecular forces to overcome gravitational forces.
